text: One of the most influential missionary orders in the world, the Jesuits have had a profound legacy in the world’s most remote corners. And though we think of the Jesuits as being far away in exotic lands, they had a considerable mission in the Sonoran Desert of the Americas. This enormous transborder region spans parts of the Mexican state of Sonora and the U.S. state of Arizona. The pioneering work of the missionary Padre Eusebio Kino (d. 1711) was soon followed by a large number of German-speaking Jesuit missionaries during the early eighteenth century. In addition to their missionary work, the various members of the order recorded in great detail their scientific observations of the area, their encounters with native people and the many day-to-day events that occurred within the region of their mission. The Jesuits were eventually expelled from Mexico and the rest of the Americas in 1767 and were later banned by the Pope in 1773, but the vast record of their activities and observations in the Sonoran region remain. Today the written and illustrated records of these Jesuit missionaries serve as some of the most valuable and reliable historical, anthropological and scientific primary source materials for the study of the colonial period of North America and the Greater North American Borderlands.
headline: Fully Human Written
